old_claim.note: Public Library Reading Rooms provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The scoped claim is a corpus of written patient narratives, making writing the direct documentary predecessor without requiring libraries or civic inspection offices.

source_shape.source_locator: The review identifies written case histories in the Hippocratic Corpus around 400 BC and reproduces a narrative from Epidemics.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Hippocratic case histories are documentary medical narratives preserved in a written corpus.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Writing is the general recording capability required by the claim, whereas public reading rooms are later distribution infrastructure absent from the case-history mechanism.

ontology_before: Medical case notes were incorrectly routed through public library reading rooms.
ontology_after: Hippocratic case histories inherit from writing as a documentary practice without requiring a public library institution.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: writing replaces public_library_reading_rooms as the documentary predecessor.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to a specific library-based medical reading or copying program.
The cited Hippocratic material proved oral-only rather than a written corpus.
short_reason: Written case histories require writing, not public reading rooms.
